9: YHWH’s announcement of Solomon’s name to David — hinnēh bēn nôlāḏ lāḵ hûʾ yihyeh ʾîš mᵉnûḥāh wᵉhaniḥôtî lô miqqōl ʾōyᵉḇāyw missāḇîḇ kî šᵉlōmōh yihyeh šᵉmô wᵉšālôm wāšeqeṭ ʾettēn ʿal yiśrāʾēl bᵉyāmāyw, ‘Behold, a son shall be born to you who shall be a man of rest. I will give him rest from all his surrounding enemies. For his name shall be Solomon (šᵉlōmōh), and I will give peace (šālôm) and quiet to Israel in his days’ — makes the name-etymology explicit: Solomon is the peace-king. The name šᵉlōmōh derives from the root šālem/šālôm (wholeness, completeness, peace). The Chronicler’s emphasis on šālôm and mᵉnûḥāh (rest, repose) as Solomon’s defining gifts frames the temple-building as the culmination of Israel’s progress toward rest — from the wilderness wandering (no rest, no temple) through the conquest (partial rest, no temple) through David’s wars (not yet rest) to Solomon’s reign (rest, temple). Hebrews 4:9-10 invokes the same framework for the eschatological rest: ara apoleipetai sabbatismos tō laō tou theou; ho gar eiselthōn eis tēn katapausin autou kai autos katepausen apo tōn ergōn autou — ‘so then, there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God, for whoever has entered God’s rest has also rested from his works.’ Solomon’s šālôm-reign is the historical type of the eschatological rest; Jesus, son of David (Matthew 1:1) and ‘greater than Solomon’ (Matthew 12:42), is its fulfillment.
1: wayyōʾmer dāwîd zeh hûʾ bêt YHWH hāʾĕlōhîm wᵉzeh-mizbēaḥ lᵉʿōlâ lᵉyiśrāʾēl — David's declaratory sentence is the theological hinge of Chronicles. The demonstrative zeh ("this") is repeated: this place / this altar — a performative speech-act designating sacred geography. The threshing floor where fire fell and the sword sheathed is now officially identified as the permanent temple site and national altar. David does not ask for divine confirmation; the theophany of 1 Chronicles 21 was itself the designation. He interprets the events with prophetic authority.
2: gērîm (resident aliens, sojourners) were the labor pool conscripted for stone preparation. The Mosaic law protected gērîm from exploitation but did not prohibit their employment in public works; the Chronicler's matter-of-fact note about their assembly reflects the Persian-period reality that the Jerusalem temple community included non-Israelite workers integrated into the economic and social fabric of Yehud. David's procurement policy here parallels Solomon's later corvée labor organization (2 Chronicles 2:17–18), demonstrating planned consistency across the two reigns.
3: barzel lārōḇ lammismerôt daltôt haššᵉʿārîm wᵉlammaḥbᵉrôt — iron in great quantity for door-nails and joints; nᵉḥōšet lᵉʾên mišqāl — bronze beyond weighing. The enumeration of materials (iron/bronze/cedar/stone) follows the ANE royal building-account convention visible in Mesopotamian inscriptions where kings catalog their procurement to demonstrate both divine favor (abundance) and royal diligence (preparation). David's stockpiling occurs during his reign but is designated entirely for Solomon — an act of dynastic stewardship rather than personal glory.
4: Cedar from Sidon and Tyre was the prestige building material of the ancient Levant, unavailable in Israel's highlands. The Phoenician trade relationship established here by David (and formalized by Solomon with Hiram of Tyre in 2 Chr 2) reflects the economic reality that Israel's hill-country ecology could not supply the temple's architectural needs. The Chronicler's inclusion of this detail grounds the sacred project in practical geopolitics: the temple required international trade partnerships that transcended Israel's borders, foreshadowing its role as a house for all peoples.
5: David's explanation for his preparations is simultaneously humble and visionary: šᵉlōmōh bᵉnî naʿar wārāk ("my son Solomon is young and inexperienced/tender") — rāk means soft/gentle, sometimes with the nuance of vulnerability; naʿar marks life stage, not inability. The temple must be lᵉgādôl lᵉšēm wᵉlᵉtipʾeret lᵉḵol-hāʾărāṣôt — "magnificent in fame and glory to all lands" — a universal visibility claim. David's preparations are a form of protective provision: he removes the logistical burden from Solomon's shoulders so that the young king can focus on the spiritual dimensions of the task.
6: David's personal charge to Solomon begins with wayyiqrāʾ lišlōmōh bᵉnô — "he called his son Solomon" — an intimate summons preceding the formal commission. The content of the charge (vv7–16) follows the structure of a testamentary address: historical retrospective (vv7–8) → promise citation (vv9–10) → blessing (vv11) → conditional exhortation (vv12–13) → resource inventory (vv14–16). This is David's spiritual will, not merely a building brief.
7: hayyâ ʿim-lᵉḇāḇî liḇnôt bêt lᵉšēm YHWH ʾĕlōhāy — "it was in my heart to build a house for the name of YHWH my God." hayyâ ʿim-lᵉḇāḇî ("was with my heart") is the Chronicler's idiom for sincere personal desire (cf. 1 Kings 8:17, Solomon's retrospective). Intention matters in Chronicles — David's failed intention is not erased by its non-fulfillment; it is honored by YHWH's alternative arrangement. The house "for the name" (lᵉšēm) reflects the Name Theology of Deuteronomy: YHWH's presence is mediated through his name dwelling in a designated place, not through physical embodiment.
8: YHWH's prohibition is stated with full solemnity: dāmîm lārōḇ šāpaḵtā — "bloods in abundance you have shed." dāmîm (plural) denotes blood-guilt — not merely the physical act of killing but the weight of responsibility for multiple deaths. milḥāmôt gᵉdōlôt ʿāśîtā — "you have waged great wars." The prohibition is not moral condemnation of David's wars per se (the Chronicler presents many of them as YHWH-commissioned) but a fitness-for-function judgment: the temple is a house of peace (šālôm), and its builder must embody that peace. War-making and temple-building require incompatible charisms.
10: hûʾ yiḇneh bêt lišmî wᵉhûʾ yihyeh-lî lᵉḇēn wᵉʾănî lō lᵉʾāḇ — the direct citation of 2 Samuel 7:13–14 / the Davidic covenant. "He will build a house for my name and he will be my son and I will be his father" — the language of adoptive royal sonship from the Ancient Near Eastern treaty tradition where the great king describes his vassal-king as "son." The Chronicler quotes the covenant verbatim within David's speech, making the temple-building mandate a matter of covenant fulfillment rather than merely royal ambition. Solomon builds not because David wants it but because YHWH promised it.
11: David's blessing: YHWH ʿimmāḵ wᵉhiṣlaḥtā ûḇānîtā bêt YHWH ʾĕlōhêḵā kaʾăšer dibber ʿālêḵā — "may YHWH be with you so that you succeed and build the house of the LORD your God as he has spoken concerning you." The conditional structure — YHWH's presence enables success — is the Chronicler's fundamental historical principle. Every successful building or battle in Chronicles succeeds because YHWH was with the protagonist; every failure occurs in his absence. David's blessing is both paternal affection and theological instruction.
12: raq yittᵉn-lᵉḵā YHWH śēḵel ûḇînâ — "only may YHWH give you wisdom and understanding." śēḵel is practical wisdom/prudence; ḇînâ is discernment/insight. The paired terms echo Proverbs' wisdom vocabulary — the temple's construction requires the full complement of the wisdom tradition's intellectual virtues. The purpose clause — wᵉlišmōr ʾet-tôrat YHWH ʾĕlōhêḵā ("to keep the torah of YHWH your God") — makes wisdom instrumental to covenant faithfulness, not an end in itself. Solomon's later request for precisely this gift (2 Chronicles 1:10) fulfills David's blessing.
13: ʾāz taṣlîaḥ ʾim-tišmōr laʿăśôt ʾet-haḥuqqîm wᵉʾet-hammišpāṭîm — "then you will succeed if you carefully observe the statutes and ordinances." The conditional ʾāz/ʾim structure (then/if) reflects Deuteronomic covenant logic: success is not guaranteed by hereditary right or building quality but by Torah faithfulness. ḥăzaq wᵉʾĕmāṣ ʾal-tîrāʾ wᵉʾal-tēḥāt — "be strong and courageous, do not fear and do not be dismayed" — the Joshua commission (Joshua 1:6–9) applied to the temple-builder: the spiritual courage required to maintain Torah is as formidable as the courage required to conquer Canaan.
14: bᵉʿonyî hăḵînôtî lᵉḇêt YHWH — "in my distress/affliction I have prepared for the house of the LORD." ʿonî (distress, affliction, poverty) is a surprising self-description from a king who is about to enumerate staggering resources; it acknowledges that David's wars, sins, and limitations are the context of his giving. The numbers are colossal: mēʾâ ʾelep kkārim zāhāb (100,000 talents of gold) / ʾelep ʾălāpîm kkārê-kesef (a million talents of silver) — whether literal or representing an incomprehensibly vast sum, the Chronicler makes clear that the temple received the accumulated wealth of David's campaigns, transformed from the instrument of war into the material of sacred space.
15: ʿimmᵉḵā lārōḇ ʿōśê melāʾḵâ — "with you in abundance are those who do the work." David's inventory of craftsmen (stonecutters/masons/woodworkers/ḵol-ḥākām-lēḇ bᵉḵol-melāʾḵâ — "every skilled-heart person in every craft") echoes the Bezalel-and-Oholiab assembly for the tabernacle (Exodus 31:6; 1 Chronicles 36:1–2). The phrase ḥākām-lēḇ (skilled/wise of heart) is the Exodus vocabulary for craftsmen whose skill is divinely given — David frames the temple workforce in tabernacle terms, establishing the spiritual continuity between Moses' wilderness tent and Solomon's permanent house.
16: zāhāḇ kesef wᵉnᵉḥōšet ûḇarzel ʾên mispār — "gold, silver, bronze, and iron beyond counting." The fourfold metal formula (gold/silver/bronze/iron) reflects the descending scale of cultic and material value from the innermost sanctuary (gold) to the outer structure (iron). qûm wᵃʿăśēh wîhî YHWH ʿimmāḵ — "rise and do it, and may YHWH be with you." The imperative charge is brief and absolute: all preparation is complete, divine presence is invoked, the command is given. David's last word to Solomon on the temple is not another exhortation but a departure — he entrusts the work.
17: David extends the temple charge to all Israel's leaders: wayyṣaw dāwîd lᵉḵol-śārê yiśrāʾēl laʿzōr lišlōmōh bᵉnô — "David commanded all the leaders of Israel to support his son Solomon." The royal succession is presented as a community project rather than a dynastic transfer: the leaders are not merely informed of the succession but commissioned to actively support the new king in the temple project. This democratic dimension of the Chronicles theology — shared communal investment in the sacred project — will climax in 1 Chr 29's freewill-offering assembly.
18: The rhetorical questions construct Israel's covenant security as the basis for action: hălōʾ YHWH ʾĕlōhêkem ʿimmāḵem ("Is not YHWH your God with you?") / ûheniḥ lāḵem missāḇîḇ ("Has he not given you rest on every side?") — the mᵉnûḥâ (rest) from enemies is both the political precondition for temple-building and the theological meaning of Solomon's reign (1 Chronicles 22:9). nātan bᵉyāḏî ʾet-yōšᵉḇê hāʾāreṣ — YHWH has already given the land's inhabitants into David's hand; the military work is done. What remains is the sacred work.
19: The climactic charge: ûttᵉnû lᵉḇaḇḵem wᵉnapšᵉḵem liḏrōš lYHWH ʾĕlōhêḵem — "give your heart and soul to seeking YHWH your God." lēḇāḇ (heart, will, intellect) + nepeš (soul, life force) — the total-person dedication formula (Deuteronomy 6:5; 1 Chronicles 30:6) applied to the temple enterprise. qûmû ûḇᵉnû ʾet-miqdaš YHWH — "rise and build the sanctuary of YHWH" — the final imperative uses miqdāš (holy place, sanctuary) rather than bêt (house), shifting from architectural to sacred register. Bringing the ark and holy vessels into the sanctuary is the goal: not architecture for its own sake but the dwelling-place of YHWH's name among his people.
